highlights: A critical study of the phenomena of religious syncretism in Angkor Cambodia The religion of Angkor Cambodia (9th to 14th century C.E.) is presented, in a recurrent, quasi-institutionalised manner, as a “syncretism” of various religious components: Brahmanism, Buddhism and indigenous religions. Drawing from more than a decade of field and archival research, this monograph concerns Cambodian cultural history and historiography, with an ultimate aim of broadening and deepening bases for understanding the Cambodian Theravadin politico-cultural complex. The book takes the form of an interdisciplinary analysis of performative and representational strategies for constituting social collectivities, largely developed at Angkor.
